summary: Gruber revisits Spielberg's E.T. after decades, watching it with his young son, and finds it even more impressive as an adult. He marvels at Spielberg's filmmaking craft, the pitch-perfect child performances, and the film's honest portrayal of childhood. He highlights how the film creates tension without true villains and embraces Clarke's dictum about advanced technology being indistinguishable from magic. He contrasts E.T.'s natural, believable child actors with Spielberg's later, less successful work with kids in Jurassic Park. The piece is a warm, personal appreciation that doubles as sharp film criticism.
key_points: E.T. creates tension and conflict without any true villains — the government agents are just people doing their jobs, shot as faceless silhouettes to establish menace without sinister characterization
The film embodies Arthur C. Clarke's dictum by treating E.T.'s alien powers as magic rather than attempting pseudoscientific explanation, unlike George Lucas's midi-chlorian approach
E.T. stands out among children's films for accurately evoking what it actually feels like to be a kid, with genuine, believable child performances
Spielberg's ability to direct child actors declined between E.T. and Jurassic Park, where the kids were so grating Gruber rooted for the raptors
money_quotes: Good lord what a magnificent picture.
There are no bad guys in the film, and yet it's filled with tension and conflict.
They're just guys doing their jobs, but yet we know to root against them, because they don't get it — they don't understand what we (through Elliott) do.
They're indistinguishable from magic and so Spielberg treats them as magic. (George "Midi-chlorian" Lucas could learn a lesson here.)
E.T. stands out as the rare film that accurately and deeply evokes what it actually feels like to be a kid.
Compare and contrast to the brother and sister in Jurassic Park, who were so grating that I found myself rooting for the raptors.
The only negative feeling the kids in E.T. evoke is jealousy, insofar as they make me wish, just as much today as in 1982, that E.T. had come to my house instead of theirs.
key_insight: E.T. endures because Spielberg trusted the emotional truth of childhood and treated the inexplicable as magic rather than demanding explanation.
